question: If a county judge has not set a speed limit on an Arkansas county road, the speed limit is:
choices: 40 mph
5 mph
45 miles per hour
55 miles per hour
explanation: Unposted county roads in Arkansas default to 40 mph. Going more than 15 mph over a county-road limit is a Class C misdemeanor.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 26 — Speed Limits on County Roads (27-51-216)

question: Speed limits are posted for ideal conditions. In rain, ice, or heavy traffic you must:
choices: Hold the posted limit exactly, since the posted number is the legally required speed in every condition
Slow to a safe speed — you can be ticketed for driving the limit if it's too fast for conditions
Keep pace with the surrounding traffic no matter how poor the road gets
Switch on your hazard flashers and continue driving at the full posted limit until conditions improve
explanation: Drivers must adjust to current conditions. Even at the posted limit, you can get a ticket for traveling too fast for the conditions.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 58 — Speed Limits

question: About how many feet does it take an average passenger car traveling 55 mph to come to a complete stop?
choices: 100 ft
150 ft
225 feet
335 ft
explanation: An average car at 55 mph needs about 225 feet of total stopping distance (reaction + braking). A loaded tractor-trailer needs more than 335 feet — the length of a football field.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 54 — Adjusting the Vehicle Speed

question: At night you should drive at a speed that lets you stop within the distance your headlights show you — about:
choices: 100 feet, about 30 mph
1,000 feet, any speed
Headlights have no real bearing on a safe speed
400 feet, which means around 50 mph
explanation: Headlights let you see about 400 feet ahead. Drive at a speed that allows you to stop within that distance — around 50 mph. In rain or fog with under 200 feet of visibility, keep it to 30 mph or less.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 58 — Visibility and Reaction (Darkness)

question: What rule does Arkansas teach for checking your following distance behind the car ahead?
choices: The two-second rule — count from when the car ahead passes a fixed point until you reach it
The car-length rule — leave one full car length of following space for every 10 mph of your speed
The license-plate rule — stay close behind the car ahead so you can read its plate
The five-second rule — keep a five-second gap behind the car ahead in every driving condition
explanation: Use the two-second rule: when the rear of the vehicle ahead passes a stationary point, count 'one-thousand-one, one-thousand-two.' If you pass the point before you finish, you're too close. Use four seconds in bad conditions.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 59 — Following Distance

question: Another driver is tailgating you on a multi-lane road. The best response is to:
choices: Brake firmly and suddenly to warn the tailgater to drop back
Move into the lane to your right when it is clear and let them pass
Speed up well past the limit to open more space in front of you
Tap your brake pedal over and over to signal them to back off
explanation: Never slow down too quickly to discourage a tailgater — it increases the risk of being hit from behind. Move right and let them by; with no right lane, wait until the path ahead is clear and reduce speed gradually.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 60 — The Safety Cushion (Being Tailgated)

question: Going much slower than the other traffic on a road:
choices: Is always the safest possible choice you can make in absolutely any kind of traffic situation you ever meet
Is the slow, cautious speed that Arkansas law actually requires every driver on the road to use after dark
Causes traffic to back up and can be just as bad as speeding — pull over and let vehicles pass
Is against the law and is never allowed under any circumstances on any Arkansas road at any time
explanation: The safest speed in traffic is the same speed as other vehicles. If cars accumulate behind you, pull over and allow them to pass.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 56 — Adjusting to the Flow of Traffic

question: When merging onto an interstate highway, your speed should be:
choices: Kept well below the posted speed limit so you can merge safely
As fast as your car can possibly go to get ahead of traffic
Held at exactly 45 mph however fast the other traffic moves
The same speed as the other traffic — use the ramp to build up speed
explanation: Enter at the same speed as traffic. Use the ramp to reach traffic speed, never drive to the end of the ramp and stop, and check your blind spot over your shoulder before merging. You need about a four-second gap.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 56 — Merging With Traffic

question: The 'Four Second Sight Distance Rule' tells you to:
choices: Pick a stationary object as far ahead as you can clearly see — if you reach it before counting to four, you're driving too fast for the distance you can see
Always keep a steady gap of exactly four full car lengths behind the vehicle ahead of you at all times, no matter how fast or slow you happen to be traveling at the moment
Come to a complete stop and wait a full four seconds before you proceed again at every single stop sign and every red light you happen to reach anywhere along your route
Finish every pass of another vehicle within four seconds of the moment you first pull out of your own lane and into the lane beside you, however long the vehicle may be
explanation: Never drive faster than the distance you can see. Count one-one-thousand to four-one-thousand toward the farthest object you can clearly see; reaching it early means slow down. At night, use it with your headlights.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 58 — Sight-Distance Rule

question: If rain or fog keeps you from seeing more than 200 feet ahead, you should drive no faster than:
choices: 50 miles per hour
45 miles per hour
30 mph
20 miles per hour
explanation: Under 200 feet of visibility → 30 mph or less. If you can't see the road ahead at all, pull over in a safe place and wait for the weather to clear.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 58 — Rain, Fog, or Snow

question: When should you increase your following distance to FOUR seconds?
choices: Only when you happen to be driving through a clearly posted active school zone during school hours
On slippery roads, when towing, in poor visibility, or when following motorcycles or vehicles that block your view
Only while you are traveling on a multi-lane interstate highway at the higher speeds those roads allow
Never, because a following distance of two seconds gives you plenty of room in every single condition
explanation: Two seconds is the minimum for ideal conditions. Use four when stopping takes longer or seeing ahead is harder: slippery roads, towing, bad weather, motorcycles ahead, or big vehicles blocking your view.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 59 — Distance and Reaction

question: To pass safely at 55 mph on a two-lane road, you need a gap in oncoming traffic of about:
choices: 10 seconds — you and the oncoming car will cover over 1,600 feet (1/3 mile)
3 seconds, since the two cars close the distance slowly even at speed
5 seconds, which leaves both vehicles plenty of room to clear the pass
30 seconds, since passing at full speed takes a very long time
explanation: At 55 mph you need about a 10-second gap and sight distance: combined, you and oncoming traffic travel over 1,600 feet — a third of a mile. If you're not sure there's time, wait.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 62 — Oncoming Vehicles

question: You should never pass when you are within ____ of a hill or curve.
choices: 100 feet
1 mile
five hundred feet
1/3 of a mile
explanation: Anytime a hill or curve blocks your view, assume an oncoming vehicle is there — never start a pass within 1/3 mile of a hill or curve.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 62 — Hills and Curves

question: After passing at night, it is safe to return to the driving lane when:
choices: You have stayed out in the passing lane for at least five full seconds
The driver of the car you just passed flashes their headlights at you twice
You can see both headlights of the passed vehicle in your rear-view mirror
You have moved a single car length ahead of the vehicle that you passed
explanation: Leave enough room before pulling back in: at night, seeing both of the passed vehicle's headlights in your rear-view mirror means you have cleared it safely.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 63 — Lane Obstructions

question: Arkansas law says the LEFT lane of a multilane highway may be used:
choices: By any vehicle at any time it wishes, exactly the same as the other lanes on the highway
Only when passing, when other lanes are closed or unsafe, or when preparing to exit on the left
Only by passenger cars and never by large trucks, which must stay out of the far-left lane
Only after dark, once the daytime traffic on the highway has thinned out and the road is clear
explanation: Under 27-51-301, don't cruise in the left lane: it's for passing, for when other lanes are closed/undrivable, or when exiting left.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 63 — Left Lane for Passing Only

question: When you need to cross several lanes of traffic, you should:
choices: Take one lane at a time
Cross them all in one move
Wait for all lanes clear
Drive on the road shoulder
explanation: Change one lane at a time, checking mirrors and blind spot for each move. Keep a four-second gap when merging or changing lanes.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 61 — Merging

question: To avoid last-minute braking, how far ahead should you be looking when you drive?
choices: About 5 seconds ahead — roughly the length of one city block when you are in town
A full 30 seconds ahead of your car at all times, no matter what road you happen to be driving on
Just past the rear bumper of the car directly in front of you in your own travel lane right now
At least 15 seconds ahead — about one city block in town, or one-quarter mile on highways
explanation: Look at least 15 seconds down the road: one city block in the city, a quarter mile on highways and interstates. The leading cause of car crashes is inattention.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 48 — Stay Alert (Looking Ahead)

question: What is the leading cause of car crashes?
choices: Heavy bad weather
Badly worn brakes
Inattention
Speeding
explanation: Inattention is the leading cause of crashes. Defensive driving starts with staying alert and keeping your eyes fixed on the road ahead.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 48 — Stay Alert

question: In an Arkansas work zone with workers present and warning signs posted, fines for moving traffic violations are:
choices: The same amount it would cost you anywhere else
Doubled — and depending on the violation, can be as high as $5,000
Reduced below the normal fine because workers are present
Waived completely for any first-time offender
explanation: All moving violations — speeding, reckless driving, distracted driving — carry DOUBLE fines in work zones, up to $5,000. Mobile work zones count too.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 18-19 — Work Zone Fines Double

question: You see signs warning of a lane closure in a work zone ahead. When should you merge?
choices: At the very last possible moment, right before the closed lane ends
As soon as it is safe to do so — ideally a mile or more before the closure
Only at the exact point where a flagger waves traffic into the open lane
After you have driven past the slower vehicles already lined up to wait
explanation: Merge early — ideally a mile or more before the closure — and be patient. Rear-end collisions are common in work zones, so keep your following distance.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 20 — Work Zone Q&A (Merge Early)

question: A work zone has no reduced speed limit posted. You should:
choices: Drive through the whole work zone at a slow and steady 10 mph the entire way
Cut your speed down to half of the normally posted limit for that road no matter what
Obey the normally posted speed limit, staying prepared for the unexpected
Just follow the vehicle ahead of you at whatever speed it happens to travel
explanation: Reduced work-zone limits are clearly posted. With no reduced limit, the normal posted limit applies — but stay ready for sudden stops, equipment, and changing patterns.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 17 — Work Zone Speed Limits

question: How does Arkansas use speed enforcement cameras?
choices: At every signalized traffic-light intersection in the towns and cities across the state
On all rural state highways, where the cameras catch and ticket any driver over the limit
Nowhere at all, because state law in Arkansas bans every form of automated camera enforcement
In interstate work zones — an officer observes violations by camera and pulls the violator over as they exit the zone
explanation: Cameras in interstate work zones let an officer wait at the end of the zone instead of inside it — safer for everyone, and you still get pulled over.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 19 — Work Zone Speed Limit

question: When preparing to pass on a two-lane road, position your vehicle:
choices: Two to three seconds behind the vehicle you plan to pass
As close to the other car's bumper as you safely can
A full ten seconds back from the vehicle you mean to pass
Already partway into the oncoming lane
explanation: Prepare to pass from two to three seconds back: check mirrors, blind spot, and oncoming traffic; confirm safe passing distance; signal — then accelerate into the passing lane.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 62 — Passing Procedures

question: How many vehicles is it safe to pass at one time on a two-lane road?
choices: As many vehicles as you can fit in before the oncoming traffic arrives
One — do not count on having enough time to pass several cars at once
Two vehicles at a time, as long as both of them are traveling quite slowly
Three vehicles at once whenever you are on a long and open straightaway
explanation: As a rule, pass only ONE vehicle at a time. Passing several cars at once gambles on time and space you can't be sure you have.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 61 — Passing Distance

question: Total stopping distance is made up of:
choices: The braking distance alone, from the moment the brakes grab
The open space between you and the car ahead as you brake
The distance traveled during your reaction time PLUS the braking distance
Only the distance the car keeps rolling after the wheels have fully locked up
explanation: Total stopping distance = reaction distance (while you perceive the hazard and move your foot) + braking distance. That's why it grows quickly with speed — and why a loaded truck needs far more room than a car.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 54, 58 — Adjusting the Vehicle Speed

question: About how many feet does an average passenger car need to stop completely from 40 mph?
choices: About 75 ft
About 225 ft
Roughly 335 feet
About 124 feet
explanation: At 40 mph total stopping distance is about 124 feet; at 55 mph it grows to about 225 feet. Higher speed means much longer stopping distance.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 58 — Visibility and Reaction

question: What is the safest speed to drive in traffic?
choices: The same speed as the other vehicles traveling in your direction
As fast as the posted speed limit will legally allow you to drive
About five mph slower than everyone else on the road around you
Whatever speed keeps you driving alone with no cars near you
explanation: Vehicles moving in the same direction at the same speed are least likely to collide. Going faster means constant passing (each pass risks a crash); going much slower backs up traffic.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 56 — Adjusting to the Flow of Traffic (Keeping Pace)

question: According to the study guide, driving faster than the flow of traffic to save time:
choices: Saves you a great deal of travel time over nearly every trip
Saves no more than a few minutes an hour — while greatly increasing your crash risk
Is the clear mark of a skilled and efficient highway driver
Is perfectly fine to do as long as you stay under the limit
explanation: Speeding does not save more than a few minutes an hour, yet each pass you make adds a chance of a collision. Slow down and keep pace with traffic.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 56 — Keeping Pace

question: You are about to take an exit from a high-speed roadway. You should:
choices: Slow down well before the exit, in the main travel lane
Stop at the very start of the ramp and check for traffic
Not slow down until you have moved onto the exit ramp
Speed up steadily as you move along and off of the exit ramp
explanation: Stay at the speed of traffic and don't slow down until you are on the exit ramp — braking early in the travel lane surprises drivers behind you. Use your turn signal to show your intentions.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 56 — Exiting Traffic

question: Farm tractors, animal-drawn vehicles, and roadway maintenance vehicles usually travel:
choices: 25 mph or less — and should display a slow-moving-vehicle emblem on the rear
At the full posted speed limit, keeping pace with the rest of the traffic around them
Faster than the surrounding traffic so that they can clear the road quickly enough
Only along the road's shoulder and never within any of the regular travel lanes
explanation: These vehicles travel 25 mph or less and display the orange-red slow-moving-vehicle triangle. Be ready to slow well in advance, since slowing suddenly can cause a crash.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 57 — Slow-Moving Traffic

question: When merging into traffic, you should enter:
choices: The smallest gap that is available, so you can slip into the moving traffic as fast as you can
Any gap at all, because the traffic already on the road is required by law to let you in
The travel lane only after you have first come to a complete stop at the end of the ramp
A gap big enough to give you a cushion of space — never try to squeeze into a small gap
explanation: You need about a 4-second gap to merge. Don't try to merge into a small gap — it can quickly become even smaller. Never drive to the end of the ramp and stop.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 56, 61 — Merging With Traffic

question: You are judging whether you have time to pass on a two-lane road. An oncoming vehicle far in the distance:
choices: Is always safe to pull out in front of and pass before, no matter how far off in the distance it is
Is always moving along far more slowly than it actually appears to be moving as it draws nearer toward you over time
Can appear to be standing still — if you can see it getting closer, it may be moving too fast for you to pass
Can be safely ignored as you pass for as long as it has not yet flashed its headlights to warn you off
explanation: It's hard to judge the speed of oncoming vehicles at a distance — one far away can look stationary. If you can tell it's getting closer, it may be too fast to pass safely. When unsure, wait.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 62 — Oncoming Vehicles

question: Before you begin to pass another vehicle, you must be sure that:
choices: The driver of the other car will make enough room for you
You have enough space to return to the driving lane
You can pass at least two of the cars in one move
The shoulder beside you is clear in case you need it
explanation: Never pass unless you have enough room to return to your lane. Don't count on other drivers making room — and watch for things (bikes, a narrow bridge, ice or potholes) that could cost you your passing space.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 63 — Lane Obstructions

question: You are following a large truck, van, or a vehicle towing a trailer that may not be able to see you. You should:
choices: Reduce your speed and fall back so you can see ahead and the driver can see you
Stay up close behind it so the driver knows for certain you are there
Move over and pass it on the right-hand side just as soon as you possibly can
Keep your high-beam headlights flashing at it steadily without a pause
explanation: Drivers of trucks, buses, vans, or vehicles pulling trailers may not see you behind them and could stop suddenly. Reduce speed and fall back — that gives you more room to see ahead, too.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 59 — Following Drivers Who Cannot See Your Vehicle

question: Posted speed limits are set based on:
choices: How fast the great majority of the drivers using that particular road would really like to be able to go
The width of the road's paved shoulder and nothing else at all about the actual design of the road itself
A single flat statewide number that the state of Arkansas applies to each and every road in the same way
The road's design, the type of vehicles using it, and the location — and they assume ideal conditions
explanation: Speed limits reflect the road's design, the vehicles that use it, and the location, and are posted for ideal conditions. In bad weather or heavy traffic you must slow below the limit.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 58 — Speed Limits

question: You merge into the MIDDLE of a four-second gap in traffic. The following distance between you and the car behind you is now about:
choices: Four seconds
One second
Two seconds
6 seconds
explanation: If you move into the middle of a four-second gap, you and the vehicle behind you each end up with about a two-second following distance. Aim to keep a four-second gap when merging or changing lanes.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 61 — Merging
